I orchards, vineyards. factories end


towns, silent carriers of light and
power-and therefore water--to tho


whole community.


Let me explain. This garden liz
all man-made. There is no rain-in
the season when rain is needed
| and there Is no water from the
rivers for hundreds Of the orchards


which are now bearing crops. The
! waters of Kings, Kaweah and Tu-
lare rivers were long ago diverted
for the orchards farther up NE can
yon. The water for these trees all
comes from wells-all is pumped.
And the pumps are run by motors
driven by electric power sold by
this one concern whose wires and
poles we Sc. Stop the electric flow
and without some power to take


its place. every ranch would be-
come a >desert waste.


But what business is this Of
yours or mine ? Just this. The
powe which feeds this valley is de.


veloped in those regions of the
earth which still belong to you and
me. 1t comes from streams which
no man owns-from the trickling
| waters in the mountain tops which
are the heritage of all. The motors
which generate the electricity for
all those pumps and lights are
driven by an energy which is still
a part of the lands and forests


which have not passed to private
ownership. The federal govern
ment Is their trustee, and congress
| is the chief agent to administer
the trust; congress and the federal
executive departments, the presi-
dent, the secretary of agriculture,
the chief of the forestry depart.
ment, and the secretary of the in
terror.


But suppose congress betrays it's
trust? Congress has done it in
many luStduCCo. OuppOoc d COlllpld-
cent senator slips through bill
which gives perpetual ownership in
such waters to the private corpor-
alton? Whence is the appeal of the
orchardman who sees his streams
dried up or his living taxed from
under his feet in extortionate rates
for power?


To you and me of the distant city
and state. We are our brothers
keeper His only hope is in friend
ly interest and an honest admins
tration of the government power
which will step in between him and
the selfish corporation, insisting
that there be fair dealing be-
tween him and the water power
landlord.
